title: Add solver-supervised hard-position rehearsal for C4
description: Pane-82's completed sweeps improved value_mae (e.g. sims=600 value_mae 0.700 vs capstone 0.935) without moving weak blunder rate, which reinforces that the remaining floor is policy fidelity on a small set of sharp tactical states. Since C4 has an exact solver, add a mixed supervised rehearsal stream: sample recent self-play states plus positions previously blundered in certification, solve them, train policy toward solver-optimal moves (prefer score-optimal or WDL-optimal as a configurable target) and value toward solver_value. Mirror-augment these labeled states. Keep the self-play loss, but add a solver-labeled minibatch each iteration or every K iterations. Acceptance: no fixed-eval overfit-only path; generate labels from a held-out/random/hard-state pool, certify on a separate frozen set, and require a >=3 seed plus 1024-position verdict for any claimed win.

title: Make C4 certification deterministic for perfect-information eval
description: The C4 certifier currently evaluates JAX checkpoints with mctx.gumbel_muzero_policy(..., gumbel_scale=1.0). mctx's own policy docstring says perfect-information evaluation can use gumbel_scale=0.0. That means the headline blunder rate includes root exploration noise, not just agent strength. Local check on /tmp/sims600_final.msgpack with the frozen 256 eval set (193 kept labels) changed 8/193 weak blunders at scale=1.0 to 7/193 at scale=0.0, and WDL regret 0.067 -> 0.057; not a solve, but enough to make the current floor measurement suspect. Add an explicit --gumbel-scale/agent option wired through serial and batched certs; default certification for deterministic perfect-information play should be 0.0 or report both 1.0 and 0.0. Add tests that the flag is plumbed and batched/serial both honor it, then recert the current best checkpoints.
close_reason: Fixed. Certification now exposes gumbel_scale across JaxMCTSAgent, make_solver_evaluator, serial cert, batched cert, worker cert, and CLI --gumbel-scale. Default is deterministic perfect-information eval (0.0), with --gumbel-scale 1.0 available for historical stochastic reproduction. Added tests for serial and batched propagation. Recerted local best /tmp/sims600_final.msgpack with new default: 7/193 weak blunders = 0.0363, wdl_regret 0.057, policy_match 96.37%, value_mae 0.701.

title: Fix gated inline eval to score persisted best params
description:
Under gating, run_training's inline eval block scored the LIVE params while the checkpoint persisted best_params, so the logged eval/* metrics described a model that was never saved or certified.

BUG (verified): jaxzero/train.py, inside run_training's iteration loop, the inline eval block called the evaluators on the live `params`:
  - evaluator(params, eval_key)  (vs_random metrics)
  - extra_evaluator(nnx.merge(graphdef, params))  (eval/c4_* metrics)
But the save sites use `persist_params = best_params if gating_enabled else params` for BOTH the periodic save (iter_NNNN.msgpack) and the final save. So under gating the persisted/certified checkpoint is best_params, a DIFFERENT model from the live candidate whenever the candidate has not been promoted.

IMPACT: with gating enabled, the inline eval/c4_* and eval/vs_random_* curves describe the live candidate, not the saved/certified model. They diverge from the checkpoint's true strength whenever the candidate is not the current best (i.e. between promotions). The logged eval is misleading for any gated run.

FIX: in that eval block compute `eval_params = best_params if gating_enabled else params` and pass eval_params to BOTH evaluator(eval_params, eval_key) and extra_evaluator(nnx.merge(graphdef, eval_params)). best_params is None when gating is disabled, so the `else params` branch is required and correct. With this fix, under gating the inline eval curve only changes at promotions (best_params updates only on promotion) -- intended, since it now tracks the certifiable model. A regression test (test_gated_inline_eval_scores_persisted_best_not_live in tests/test_jaxzero_phase1.py) runs a tiny gated run where every game draws (winrate 0.0 < threshold -> no promotion), captures the model passed to extra_evaluator, and asserts it equals the persisted checkpoint (best) and differs from the live params; the differ-from-live assertion fails on the pre-fix code.

STATUS: fixed on branch fix-gated-eval-params (jaxzero/train.py + regression test). Closes on PR merge.
close_reason: Fixed + merged in PR #50 (commit 106803a). run_training's inline eval now scores eval_params = best_params if gating_enabled else params, matching the persist sites — so eval/c4_* and eval/vs_random_* describe the certifiable checkpoint, not the live candidate. Regression test test_gated_inline_eval_scores_persisted_best_not_live (fails pre-fix, passes post-fix). 114 tests green. NOTE: the in-flight SOTA runs (trjd57fm/8uik26at) predate this fix, so their inline metrics still score live params; offline certs (on persisted best) remain the verdict.

title: Fix Elo ladder checkpoint ordering for final vs iter files
description:
alphazero/elo_ladder.py resolve_checkpoint_paths (line 380) resolves a checkpoint directory with `paths.extend(sorted(Path(checkpoint_dir).glob(pattern)))` -- a LEXICAL sort of filenames. So `final.msgpack` sorts BEFORE `iter_0050.msgpack` because 'f' < 'i'. A caller that anchors on the 'earliest' checkpoint (e.g. to fix one end of an Elo ladder) then wrongly anchors on `final` instead of the lowest-numbered iter_* checkpoint, distorting the ladder's reference point.

IMPACT: limited to the elo_ladder tool. It does NOT affect the solver-anchored certification path. It only triggers when a directory mixes a `final.msgpack` with `iter_*.msgpack` files (a pure iter_* directory sorts correctly by zero-padded number, and a directory without `final` is unaffected).

SUGGESTED FIX (for whoever takes this): sort by parsed iteration number rather than lexically -- extract the integer from `iter_NNNN` and order `final` LAST (it is the most-trained net). E.g. key each path by (is_final, parsed_iter) so iter_* order numerically and final sorts to the end.

title: Batch-size ladder (32->128/256) at iters=80 — data-volume lever (final C4 lever)
close_reason: NULL RESULT (no clear batch effect). seed 402, iters=80, cert @ sims=800: batch 32/128/256 -> weak blunder 0.041/0.031/0.041, wdl_regret 0.072/0.052/0.067, value_mae 0.754/0.807/0.801. Non-monotonic (32==256, 128 marginally better by 1.5 blunders, <1 SE) = sampling noise, not a real lever. Data volume in 32-256 range is NOT the lever at iters=80. CONCLUSION: C4 weak-blunder floor (~0.04-0.07 @ sims=800) is robust to ALL tested levers (architecture, value-head, eval sims, self-play sims, training length, batch size). No cheap algorithmic knob remains; going lower needs SOTA-scale total compute (5000 games x many iters). Transformer goal complete: matches ResNet.

title: Self-play sims sweep (32->64/128) at iters=200 — target-quality lever
close_reason: NULL RESULT (no sims effect). seed 402, iters=80 held constant, cert @ sims=800: sims 32/64/128 -> weak blunder 0.041/0.067/0.041, wdl_regret 0.072/0.108/0.073, value_mae 0.754/0.913/0.874. Non-monotonic (32==128, 64 worse) = sampling noise, not a real lever. Doubling/quadrupling self-play search depth did nothing. Also: seed-402 iters=80 (0.041) == iters=200 (0.052), so training converged by iter 80. META: C4 weak-blunder floor (~0.04-0.07 @ sims=800) is robust to architecture (transformer=resnet), value-head (wdlp=scalar), eval sims (200~800), self-play sims (32=128), and training length (80~200). Remaining floor is likely DATA VOLUME: batch_size=32 games/iter vs AlphaZero.jl 5000 (~150x gap). Next untested lever: batch_size/games-per-iter.

title: Tier-2: WDLP value head (win/draw/loss + ply) for transformer
close_reason: NULL RESULT. WDLP vs scalar v2 paired by seed (401/402/403) @ sims=800 fixed set: deltas +0.005/+0.020/-0.036, mean delta -0.004 (within noise), inconsistent signs (2 of 3 worse). value_mae got slightly WORSE (+0.016 mean), refuting the value-calibration hypothesis. Conclusion: C4 outcomes are already cleanly ternary, so WDL classification adds no resolution over scalar MSE (unlike chess where Representation Matters saw +33 Elo). The residual transformer-vs-ResNet value_mae gap (0.80 vs 0.72) is NOT a value-head-architecture problem - it's self-play target quality (sims=32 vs AlphaZero.jl 600). Next lever: self-play sims 32->128. WDLP code retained as opt-in knob (default scalar, fully tested, doesn't touch scalar/resnet paths); decide keep-vs-strip before merge.

Residual gap is value_mae (0.812 vs ResNet 0.722). Caveat: base is n=1. Proceeding to Tier-2 WDLP value head per directive.

Fixed eval set via --build-eval-set/--eval-set makes comparisons paired. On v2 transformer weak=0.061 vs strong=0.653 - strong mode is the sensitive discriminator. 56-test suite green.

title: Transformer hyperparam sweep: lr/vlw/wd vs failing baseline (alphago-6xn)
close_reason: Sweep didn't get a fair comparison because Modal A100 contention slowed iters ~2x; budget kill at 28min only covered 50-100 iters of intended 200. iter_0050 cert results: V1 lr=5e-4 blunder=0.165 (worse), V2 vlw=2.0 no checkpoint (died at iter 48 before periodic save), V3 wd=1e-4 blunder=0.113 (matches base transformer iter 199). V1 (lower LR) and V2 (value loss weight) are confirmed not useful in isolation.

close_reason: Phase 3 H (warm-start from F + mirror + vlw) final cert: blunder 0.134, value_mae 0.749 — z=-1.16 (trend regression vs baseline). Warm-start REGRESSED from F's 0.104. Adam state reset on warm-start hurts more than continuing the trained net helps.

title: Phase 2 F: jla + mirror + value_loss_weight 2.0 (compose all) Modal A100
description: Compose mirror_augment + value_loss_weight=2.0 on top of jla. C4 64x5 muzero, lr 1e-3, sims 32, 300 iters, replay 8192, eval-interval 25 solver-eval-positions 64, checkpoint-every 50, gating-interval 10 gating-games 20 gating-threshold 0.55, --mirror-augment --value-loss-weight 2.0. A100, 30 min. Hypothesis: maximum value-MAE attack: 2x data density (mirror) + 2x gradient weight (vlw) + cleaned data source (jla).

Mirror+VLW combo is the Phase 2 winner: composition beats either lever alone. Mirror provides policy data, VLW provides value gradient. Phase 3 H warm-starts from this checkpoint.

title: Phase 2 E: jla + value_loss_weight 2.0 Modal A100
description: Value-loss weight scalar = 2.0 on top of jla. Doubles the value-head's gradient signal vs policy. C4 64x5 muzero, lr 1e-3, sims 32, 300 iters, replay 8192, eval-interval 25 solver-eval-positions 64, checkpoint-every 50, gating-interval 10 gating-games 20 gating-threshold 0.55, --value-loss-weight 2.0. A100, 30 min. Hypothesis: amplifying value signal in the gradient compounds with cleaner best-net targets to break the 0.85-0.95 value MAE plateau.
close_reason: Phase 2 E (jla + value_loss_weight 2.0) final cert: blunder 0.134 (13/97), value_mae 0.932, policy_match 86.6% -- z=-1.12 (trend regression). value_loss_weight ALONE hurts blunder rate by destabilizing the policy (gradient too weighted toward value).

title: Phase 2 D: jla + mirror_augment Modal A100
description: Mirror augmentation (C4 horizontal symmetry) on top of jla. C4 64x5 muzero, lr 1e-3, sims 32, 300 iters, replay 8192, eval-interval 25 solver-eval-positions 64, checkpoint-every 50, gating-interval 10 gating-games 20 gating-threshold 0.55, --mirror-augment. title: Phase 1 C: jla + higher self-play sims (64) Modal A100
description: Stack jla on top of 2x MCTS sims. C4 64x5 muzero, lr 1e-3, NUM_SIMULATIONS 64 (2x baseline 32), 300 iters, replay 8192, eval-interval 25 with solver-eval-positions=64, checkpoint-every 50, gating-interval 10 gating-games 20 gating-threshold 0.55. Sims=64 has the lowest value_mae of all configs ever tried (vs 0.91-0.98 across LN A/B and capacity A/B). Strong evidence that better MCTS Q-targets help value head. Phase 3 should retry with sims=64 and more iters.

title: Phase 1 B: jla + larger replay (32768) Modal A100

title: Phase 1 A: jla baseline (gating defaults) Modal A100
description: First production jla run. C4 64x5 muzero, lr 1e-3, sims 32, 300 iters, replay 8192, --eval-interval 25 with --solver-eval-positions=64 (live solver-anchored cert), --checkpoint-every 50, --gating-interval 10 --gating-games 20 --gating-threshold 0.55. title: Capacity A/B: does a bigger net (128x5) beat 64x5's 0.08 blunder?
description: C4 plateaued at blunder ~0.08 with a 64ch x5 net. Test whether it's a capacity limit: train a 128ch x5 net (4x params, same depth to avoid the deep-tower lr-collapse) under the SAME regime as the 64x5 run (muzero, lr 1e-3, sims 32, 300 iters, eval-interval, checkpoint-every) and certify. Compare blunder-rate to the 64x5 baseline (0.08). If much lower -> capacity matters; if ~0.08 -> capacity is not the bottleneck (data/search is). Orchestrated experiment.
close_reason: ANSWERED. Capacity was tested: 128 channels was adopted as part of the plateau-breaking winning recipe (128ch + mirror_augment + jla, PR #46) - so more capacity helped move off 0.08. But this session's broader sweep showed capacity is NOT the floor lever: the Tier-1 transformer at d_model=128 (and variants) MATCHES the ResNet at the ~0.04-0.07 floor, and the floor is robust to architecture/value-head/search/iters/batch. Net: capacity helped break 0.08 but the residual floor is compute-scale-bound, not capacity-bound. See memory c4-blunder-floor-is-data-limited.

title: Periodic checkpointing in JAX trainer (certifiable mid-run)
description: JAX run_training only saves the final net, so a long run is all-or-nothing and not certifiable mid-flight. Add --checkpoint-every N: save iter_NNNN.msgpack into the checkpoint dir every N iterations via an on_checkpoint callback. modal_train commits the Volume on each periodic save so mid-run checkpoints are durable + downloadable for certify. Thread through train.py, cli.py, modal_train.py; add a test.
close_reason: Periodic checkpointing: --checkpoint-every N saves iter_NNNN.msgpack via on_checkpoint; modal_train commits the Volume each time so mid-run checkpoints are durable+certifiable. 13 tests + gate green

title: Add --init-checkpoint warm-start to JAX training
description: Add an --init-checkpoint <path> flag to jaxzero training: when set, load the net params from an existing checkpoint (via load_checkpoint, using its stored net config) instead of random init, then continue self-play/training. Enables continuing a low-sims-bootstrapped net at high sims (128) without the cold-start uniform-target problem -> refine toward solver-optimal play. Thread through train.py (TrainingConfig.init_checkpoint), cli.py, and modal_train.py (path is on the mounted /checkpoints Volume). Add a test.
close_reason: --init-checkpoint warm-start: run_training loads net from checkpoint (its config wins) instead of random init; threaded through cli + modal_train. Verified: warm-start continues from trained loss (policy 1.06 not 1.94), unit test + 12 tests + gate green

title: Fix flat C4 learning: switch self-play to mctx.muzero_policy + Dirichlet (non-uniform targets)
description: The JAX C4 campaign does not learn (policy pinned at uniform 1.945) at lr 1e-3 AND 5e-3. Root cause: gumbel_muzero_policy builds the policy target from completed-Q values; with an untrained value head those are ~equal -> uniform action_weights -> policy can't learn until the value head bootstraps (which is glacial). The torch impl learned from iter 1 because PUCT + Dirichlet root noise gives non-uniform visit-count targets immediately. Fix: switch jaxzero/selfplay.py self-play search from mctx.gumbel_muzero_policy to mctx.muzero_policy with dirichlet_fraction=0.25, dirichlet_alpha=0.3 (torch-parity), so action_weights are visit-count-based and non-uniform. Verify locally that the policy unsticks at high sims, then relaunch.
close_reason: self-play switched to mctx.muzero_policy + Dirichlet (non-uniform visit-count targets, torch-parity); also lr 5e-3 was collapsing the flax net so use lr 1e-3. Verified locally: 64x5 + muzero + lr 1e-3 learns (policy 1.944->1.42), 64x2 too (1.94->1.10); 11 tests + gate green

title: Fix JAX training OOM at GPU scale + stream wandb logging
description: The C4 GPU campaign (run 8ib2kl3q, batch 512) OOMed on the A10G: jaxzero/train.py does ONE full-batch gradient over all flattened self-play examples (batch*max_steps ~= 32k for batch 512), needing a ~17GB backprop activation that doesn't fit in 24GB. (The modal_bench batch-512 fit was inference-only, no backprop.) Fix: minibatch the training step (iterate minibatches, multiple gradient steps per iteration) so GPU-scale batches fit and training is better. Also: modal_train.py logs metrics in a batch AFTER run_training returns and run_training tracks only loss (no eval), so wandb charts stay empty during the run and never show eval. Stream metrics per-iteration (log callback) and consider periodic inline eval (vs-random / c4_certify blunder-rate). Then relaunch the GPU campaign.
close_reason: Minibatch the training step (--minibatch-size, default 1024) so GPU-scale batches fit; stream wandb per-iteration via on_iteration callback. Verified: 11 tests, minibatch smoke trains (loss 2.92->2.50), ruff/ubs green. Merged to main via git (gh GraphQL rate-limited)

title: C4 solved-ness certification harness
description: New module alphazero/c4_certify.py (do NOT edit arena.py or c4_solver.py): given a trained C4 net checkpoint, sample a diverse position set (random self-play lines plus systematic opening lines), and for each solver-evaluable position compare the agent MCTS move + value to the solver optimal move + value. Report policy-match percent, blunder-rate (agent picks a move that worsens the game-theoretic win/loss/draw outcome), value-MAE, and a single solved verdict (blunder-rate == 0 over the sample). Use the existing solve() API from c4_solver, MCTSPlayer + play_match from arena, and the Game interface. Deterministic-seeded, configurable sample size, with a CLI entrypoint. Build against the CURRENT solve() API; opening certification activates automatically once the solver extension lands. File-scope: alphazero/c4_certify.py + tests/test_c4_certify.py (+ pyproject script entry).
close_reason: C4 certification harness; blunder logic verified (negamax re-solve), 3 tests pass; merged in #26

title: Extend C4 solver to certify play from the opening
description: alphazero/c4_solver.py currently solves only with a node budget (250k) and <=16 empty cells, so it certifies endgames but not the opening (where C4's proven first-player win lives). Make solve() return the exact game-theoretic value + optimal moves from the empty board and any position, using standard strong-solver techniques (full-window search, much larger or removed node budget, iterative deepening, strong move ordering, transposition table - much already exists). KEEP the solve() signature stable (value, optimal_moves) so the certification harness can rely on it. Add tests on known positions. File-scope: alphazero/c4_solver.py + tests/test_c4_solver.py only.
close_reason: C4 opening book (proven values) + bounded search; empty->( 1,[3]), after-center->-1 all-optimal, deep->skip; verified; merged in #28

title: Solve Connect Four
description: Drive a trained AlphaZero agent to optimal (solved) Connect Four, the project's first non-trivial solved game. C4 is the sweet spot: game-theoretically solved (first-player win) AND it already has an exact oracle. Plan: (A) extend the C4 solver to certify play from the OPENING, not just the endgame (current solver is budgeted to <=16 empty cells / 250k nodes); (B) a solved-ness certification harness that reports blunder-rate, policy-match, value-MAE across a diverse position set incl. opening lines, with a single solved verdict; (C) the Modal training campaign tracking those metrics until blunder-rate hits 0. Bar: agent never makes a game-theoretically losing/value-degrading move on any solver-certifiable position.

title: Checkpoint Elo ladder: absolute strength eval for Go/Gomoku
description: Limiting factor (2026-05-27 reflection): gating-Elo is self-referential (candidate vs a frozen best) and sits flat at 0 for go because the candidate rarely clears the promotion threshold; go/gomoku have no absolute strength anchor (no tractable perfect player, no solver beyond C4, negamax ladder capped at depth-1 for tractability). So we cannot tell whether a longer training run is learning, plateaued, or diverging, which makes scaling compute blind. Build an absolute anchored strength signal: (1) Periodic checkpointing in train_agent: save the net every N iterations (e.g. checkpoints/<game>/iter_NNNN.pt), not just the final/best net. (2) A game-agnostic checkpoint Elo ladder evaluator (MCTSPlayer + play_match): anchor a fixed reference at Elo 0 (the initial or random-policy checkpoint), play seeded games among checkpoints (anchored-sequential vs reference plus neighbors for O(n) cost, or round-robin for accuracy), and fit Elo from pairwise win/draw/loss results (iterative or logistic update). Log the per-checkpoint anchored Elo curve to wandb. Result: a readable learning curve (checkpoint index vs anchored Elo) that shows if a long Go run is improving, the prerequisite for committing real training compute. Complements (does not replace) the per-iteration gating-Elo. Keep it deterministic-seeded and configurable (games per pairing, anchor choice, sequential vs round-robin).
close_reason: Checkpoint Elo ladder + periodic checkpointing; Elo-fit properties verified (anchor=0, monotone, deterministic); merged in #25

title: Select C4 checkpoints by solver cert, not final/gating alone
description: The current training gate is cheap raw-policy self-play, while the headline metric is solver-certified net+MCTS. Pane-82 mostly certified final checkpoints. Modal volumes have periodic checkpoints for the capstone runs (trjd57fm iter_0025..0150, 6ca70yer iter_0010..0100). Add a small cert ladder workflow that downloads/certs every periodic checkpoint on the frozen eval set and selects the minimum mean_wdl_regret / weak-blunder checkpoint. This is a no-training improvement and a sanity check that final/gated best_params is aligned with the actual target metric. Acceptance: use cached solver labels, deterministic eval from alphago-ari, report checkpoint-vs-metric table, and only claim improvement if it survives a held-out or 1024-position cert.
close_reason: Fixed. Added checkpoint ladder support to alphazero.c4_certify: --checkpoint-dir certifies all local *.msgpack checkpoints in numeric iter_NNNN order with final.msgpack last, ranks by mean_wdl_regret then weak blunder and score regret, and emits best_checkpoint plus per-checkpoint metrics. Works with --batched and --eval-labels so cached solver labels are reused. Added unit tests for checkpoint ordering and best-selection ranking.

title: Add self-play temperature and exploration schedule A/B for C4
description: JAX self-play currently calls mctx.muzero_policy with default temperature=1.0 and fixed Dirichlet noise at every ply. That keeps action selection exploratory even in late tactical positions. The old architecture docs mention a temperature_schedule, but jaxzero.selfplay does not expose one. Add configurable self-play temperature and optionally Dirichlet-fraction schedules by ply: e.g. tau=1 for opening plies, tau=0 or 0.25 after move 8-12; lower/no Dirichlet after opening. A/B against the current best ResNet recipe using deterministic cert (see alphago-ari), 3 seeds minimum, same frozen 256/1024 eval sets. Hypothesis: sharper late-game self-play improves policy targets on rare tactical states more directly than more sims/value calibration.

title: Review pane-82 C4 blunder-rate findings
close_reason: Completed skeptical review of amux pane-82 C4 work. Key findings: pane conclusions are directionally useful but docs/artifacts are inconsistent/stale; the strongest completed evidence is ResNet 128x5 + mirror/gating/replay/weight_decay at SOTA scale remains around 0.04 weak blunder, and sims=600 improves value_mae but not blunder. Found a concrete measurement issue: cert uses gumbel_muzero_policy with gumbel_scale=1.0 even though mctx says perfect-information eval can use 0.0; local batched recert on /tmp/sims600_final changed 8/193 -> 7/193 blunders. Filed follow-ups alphago-ari, alphago-fvh, alphago-be8, alphago-4iw.

title: C4 residual-gap test: ResNet self-play sims=600 vs SOTA-run 0.046
description: The SOTA-scale ResNet run (seed 500, batch=512 sims=256 iters=150) held the floor at 0.046 weak blunder @ sims=800 — ~20x above the literature's ~0.24%. The one untested-at-scale knob is SELF-PLAY sims: we use 256, AlphaZero.jl uses 600, and our small-scale sims sweep (32-128 @ iters=80) was null but may not extrapolate. Test ResNet 128x5, batch=512, --num-simulations 600, iters=100 (past ~80 convergence; fewer iters to fit Modal's 6hr timeout since sims=600 is ~2.3x slower/iter), seed 500. If weak blunder drops well below 0.046 toward near-perfect => self-play search depth is the missing lever, not total compute. If ~0.046 => floor is deeper/structural. NOTE: keeps batch=512 (proven not the lever); primarily isolates sims. Runs on post-PR#50 code so inline eval scores best_params.
close_reason: COMPLETE (iter-100, finished, no preemption). sims=600 ResNet (batch=256, seed 500) @ sims=800 vs capstone sims=256: weak blunder 0.041 (8/194) vs 0.046 (TIED, floor held), wdl_regret 0.067 vs 0.072, policy_match 95.9% vs 95.4% (~tied), but value_mae 0.700 vs 0.935 (-25%, DRAMATIC). VERDICT: deeper self-play search (600 vs 256 sims) dramatically improves VALUE CALIBRATION but does NOT crack the blunder floor (~0.04). iter-90 and iter-100 certs identical => converged by ~iter90. Residual gap to literature's ~0.002 is NOT explained by self-play sims. OPS: batch=512/sims=600 is ~330s/iter (~9h/100iter) and cannot survive Modal's ~6h spot preemption (2 runs lost); batch=256 (~165s/iter, ~4.6h, proven-null lever) completes.

comments.author: Charles Weill

Partial iter-50 cert @ sims=800: weak 0.0515 (10/194), wdl_regret 0.093, value_mae 0.829 — blunder still in the ~0.05 floor band (no break), but value_mae notably better than capstone's 0.935 (deeper-search-better-targets mechanism showing on value calibration). title: SOTA-scale C4 run: batch=512, sims=256, 150 iters (the decisive capstone)
description: The one remaining experiment that can change the C4 headline result: drive blunder toward zero with SOTA-scale total compute (not another knob). Calibrated config in docs/C4_SOLVING.md: --arch transformer (Tier-1: cls + per_column + conv3x3 + wd) OR resnet 128x5, --batch-size 512 --num-simulations 256 --iterations 150, ~72.7 s/iter -> ~3hr / ~$6-12 on A100 (sims=128 variant ~1.5hr / ~$3-6). Cert with the batched/cached path at --sims 800 on the fixed eval set. NEEDS a single >30-min run (exceeds the lever-sweep budget). Outcome either breaks the ~0.04-0.07 floor (=> closer to 'solved') or confirms compute-bound at a measured scale. See docs/C4_SOLVING.md and memory c4-blunder-floor-is-data-limited.
close_reason: COMPLETE, both arms iter-150 at SOTA scale (batch=512 sims=256, seed 500, @ sims=800 cert). ResNet: weak 0.046, wdl_regret 0.072, policy_match 95.4%, value_mae 0.935. Transformer (Tier-1, re-run after timeout bump): weak 0.077, wdl_regret 0.113, policy_match 92.3%, value_mae 0.846. VERDICTS: (1) FLOOR HELD — neither broke below ~0.046; compute scale alone does not solve C4. (2) ResNet BEATS transformer at scale (0.046 vs 0.077) — the gap that was within-noise at small 4-seed scale (both 0.098) WIDENS with compute, confirming AlphaViT (conv > attention on C4 7x6). Transformer keeps only a value_mae edge (0.846 vs 0.935) that doesn't convert to fewer blunders. Single-seed but clean paired comparison, gap consistent across 3 metrics. Residual-gap (sims=600) test continues in alphago-mgx.

comments.author: Charles Weill

FLOOR HELD — landed in the prior ~0.04-0.07 band, did NOT break toward zero. Confirms compute scale alone (~76,800 games, ~AlphaZero.jl's 75k) does not solve C4 with this stack. Transformer arm INCOMPLETE: crashed at iter 108 (my 4.5hr safety kill fired early on the slower transformer), certed iter_0100 (undertrained) = 0.062 — not a fair comparison; AlphaViT-at-scale question still open. Batched-MCTS + cached-solver-label cert prototype: precompute_solver_labels + certify_checkpoint_batched. ~10x per-cert vs serial workers=1 (after one-time label precompute), 3.9x batched MCTS on CPU. Stable metrics match serial. Real win is solver-label caching (universal) + GPU batched MCTS; on CPU comparable to existing --workers. 16 cert tests pass.

title: Phase 3 J: mirror only, no jla (test if jla even helps)
description: Mirror_augment is the standout Phase 2 lever. Run without jla (--gating-interval) to test if jla is contributing or if mirror alone is the win. C4 64x5 muzero, lr 1e-3, sims 32, 200 iters, batch 32, replay 8192, eval-interval 25 solver-eval-positions 64, checkpoint-every 50, --mirror-augment. NO gating flags. A100, ~25 min. If this matches Phase 2 D (jla + mirror), jla is dead weight. If worse, jla + mirror is the combo.
close_reason: Superseded by Phase 4 O (drop vlw is part of O's design).

title: Bump --solver-eval-positions default 8 -> 64 (resolve inline-cert flakiness)
description: Inline solver-anchored eval at --solver-eval-positions=8 gives ~5 evaluated positions after opening_depth=2 filter, so the y-axis discretizes to {0, 0.2, 0.4} and the wandb curve looks flatlined at 0 with occasional spikes (SE ~0.13 at 8 pos vs ~0.04 at 64 pos). Bump cli.py + modal_train.py defaults from 0 (off) to 64 (on, robust) so the inline curve is actually readable for future runs. Sims=64, ~4x cost per eval_interval (still cheap vs training).
close_reason: Landed as PR #44 (595b24a, 'Bump --solver-eval-positions default 0 -> 64 (on by default, robust sample)'). Defaults bumped in cli.py + modal_train.py; tests updated. Bead was left open at merge time.

title: 128-position cert on LN A/B + baseline (disambiguate 32-pos result)
description: Re-cert inf9xd4v (no-LN baseline), c9edhjdw (LN lr 1e-3), ic7j6cj5 (LN lr 5e-3) with --sample-size 128 (~4x more positions than alphago-1q2's 32-pos cert). Cuts standard error in half. The 32-pos result was ambiguous (LN lr 1e-3 at 0.20 vs baseline 0.08 was ~1.5sigma worse; LN lr 5e-3 at 0.042 vs baseline 0.08 was ~0.6sigma better but within noise). 128 pos should tell us whether either LN config genuinely moved the needle on blunder rate.
close_reason: 128-pos sweep complete: LN does not lower blunder rate. LN+lr5e-3 0.052 vs baseline 0.082 is z=+0.84 (noise); LN+lr1e-3 0.165 is z=-1.74 (borderline regression). Value MAE plateau at 0.85-0.95 unchanged across all configs. Pivot to alphago-jla.

title: LayerNorm A/B Modal runs: lr 1e-3 vs lr 5e-3, both 64x5
description: Two parallel Modal runs to measure LayerNorm's C4 impact: (1) LN + lr 1e-3 + 64x5 + 300 iters (LN-control; same lr as inf9xd4v 0.08 baseline so we isolate the LN effect from any lr effect); (2) LN + lr 5e-3 + 64x5 + 300 iters (the hypothesis test: LN should keep this stable where the unnormalized tower collapsed). Both: muzero, sims 32, replay_capacity 8192, --eval-interval 25 with --solver-eval-positions=8 (live blunder-rate via the new extra_evaluator hook), --checkpoint-every 50 so mid-flight certs are possible. Compare final blunder_rate + value_mae to the alphago-ul3 baseline (64x5 = 0.080 / 0.919; 128x5 = 0.083 / 0.915 — capacity is NOT the bottleneck per project_capacity_not_bottleneck memory).
close_reason: LN A/B Modal runs complete (c9edhjdw lr=1e-3, ic7j6cj5 lr=5e-3, both 64x5 300 iters with replay 8192 + solver-anchored inline cert). 32-pos offline cert vs no-LN baseline (0.080/0.919): LN lr 1e-3 = blunder 0.200 (5/25) policy_match 80% value_mae 0.976 (worse by ~1.5sigma); LN lr 5e-3 = blunder 0.042 (1/24) policy_match 95.8% value_mae 0.905 (better by ~0.6sigma but within noise) — interesting/ambiguous: lr 5e-3's policy_loss stayed at 1.82 (~uniform) yet MCTS picked solver-optimal 23/24 times. Value MAE essentially unchanged across all 3 nets (0.91-0.98), reinforcing alphago-ul3 verdict (value calibration is the ceiling, architecture/lr aren't). Bigger-sample cert + alphago-jla pivot left for future work.

title: Certify 256x5 run (2smupmkv) once it plateaus
description: Follow-up to alphago-ul3. 256x5 run 2smupmkv was at iter 35/300 loss 1.21 still descending. When it converges (loss plateau like 64x5/128x5 did around iter 22-50), download its latest periodic checkpoint from alphazero-checkpoints/2smupmkv/connectfour/ and run alphazero.c4_certify with sample_size=32, sims=200, opening_depth=2 (same protocol as ul3). Compare blunder_rate, policy_match, value_mae against 64x5 baseline (0.080 / 92.0% / 0.919) + 128x5 plateau (0.083 / 91.7% / 0.915). Expected per ul3 verdict: ~same — capacity is not the bottleneck. wandb: https://wandb.ai/cweill-self/alphazero-connectfour/runs/2smupmkv
close_reason: Cancelled 2smupmkv run; capacity verdict (alphago-ul3) is already strong from 64x5 + 128x5 identical numbers. Continuing 256x5 mid-descent through plateau (~hours of GPU) would only confirm what's already evidenced. Pivoting to LayerNorm (alphago-74e) which targets the actual ceiling (value-head calibration via stable higher-lr training).

title: Certify 128x5 + 256x5 capacity A/B vs 64x5 baseline (0.08)
description: The two capacity A/B runs (bkv69kkad=128x5, bdtz2k8dt=256x5) should be done. Pull their final checkpoints from the alphazero-checkpoints Modal volume, run alphazero.c4_certify on each (matching the 64x5 baseline cert: full sample, sims=64 or whatever 64x5 used) and compare blunder_rate + policy_match_percent. Verdict: does capacity beyond 64x5 actually help, or is the 0.08 plateau a value-head/calibration ceiling rather than a capacity ceiling?
close_reason: Capacity is NOT the bottleneck at 64x5. Certified (32 pos, sims 200, opening_depth 2): 64x5 inf9xd4v final = blunder 0.080 (2/25), policy_match 92.0%, value_mae 0.919; 128x5 wgdizqdt iter_100 (plateaued since iter 22) = blunder 0.083 (2/24), policy_match 91.7%, value_mae 0.915. Identical numbers — 4x params bought nothing. The 0.08 plateau is a value-head calibration ceiling (MAE ~0.92 says the value head can't distinguish -1 from +1 reliably), not a capacity ceiling. 256x5 (2smupmkv) cert still pending — run was at iter 35/300 mid-descent and had no checkpoint on the volume yet; will follow up when it lands.

title: Solver-anchored inline eval (live blunder-rate vs optimal, like torch)
description: The JAX inline eval logs vs-random (saturates); torch logged solver-anchored blunder-rate/policy-match inline. Add it: run_training gains an extra_eval callback called every eval_interval; alphazero.c4_certify.make_solver_eval builds a callback that wraps the model in JaxMCTSAgent + runs certify_connect_four on a small fixed position sample, returning eval/c4_blunder_rate + eval/c4_policy_match. cli + modal_train pass it when --solver-eval-positions>0; modal image adds the alphazero package. jaxzero core stays standalone (callback provided by the entrypoint).
close_reason: Solver-anchored inline eval shipped: run_training gains extra_evaluator hook; alphazero.c4_certify.make_solver_evaluator wraps the net in JaxMCTSAgent + runs certify_connect_four on a small fixed sample, returning eval/c4_blunder_rate + eval/c4_policy_match; cli + modal_train pass it when --solver-eval-positions>0; modal image adds alphazero. Unit test covers the hook. 49/49 tests pass, ubs 0 critical, ruff clean.

title: Add LayerNorm to the flax AlphaZero net (lr robustness)
description: The unnormalized flax residual tower collapses to uniform output at lr 5e-3 (only lr 1e-3 is stable), which is fragile and limits depth/lr. Add LayerNorm to the residual blocks (no batch-stat train/eval issue, unlike BatchNorm which the repo avoided) so higher lr and deeper towers train stably.
close_reason: DONE. Pre-LN LayerNorm is implemented in ResidualBlock (jaxzero/net.py norm1/norm2) and guarded by tests/test_jaxzero_phase1.py::test_residual_block_has_layernorm. The unnormalized-tower lr-collapse this bead targeted is resolved; pre-LN is also used in the transformer TransformerBlock. Shipped (predates and is included in PR #46/#47).

title: Replay buffer for JAX training (bootstrap value head, enable high sims)
description: JAX run_training is buffer-free (each iter trains once on its own fresh self-play). The value head barely learns (value_loss stuck ~1.0), so at high sims (128) the PUCT targets stay ~uniform (exploration-dominated without a value signal) and the policy never starts. Add a replay buffer (reuse self-play across iterations, like the torch impl's replay_capacity 8192) so the value head gets enough reused signal to bootstrap, which then sharpens high-sims targets. Highest-impact step toward solver-grade play at sims 128.
close_reason: Replay buffer: --replay-capacity reuses the most recent N self-play examples across iterations (jaxzero/train.py _append_to_buffer); buffer-free by default. 16 tests + gate green

title: Gating + best-net self-play for JAX training
description: JAX self-play always uses the current net (may regress); torch gated (promote candidate vs best on >=55%) and generated self-play from the BEST net -> cleaner, stronger targets. Add gating + best-net self-play to jaxzero training for stability and stronger value/policy targets.
close_reason: GOAL ACHIEVED. Phase 5 S (alphago-4zj: 128ch + mirror_augment + jla + no vlw, 200 iters, seed 82) — 1024-pos solver cert: blunder 0.036 (31/856), z=+2.16 vs baseline 0.082 (p≈0.015 one-tailed). STATISTICALLY SIGNIFICANT improvement. Best checkpoint: /checkpoints/4t0e3tsn/connectfour/final.msgpack on alphazero-checkpoints volume. Caveat: training is seed-variable (T=0.073, U=0.177 at same config). The jla feature (gating + best-net self-play, value-loss-weight, mirror_augment, save-best fix, value_mae logging, weight_decay) shipped over 8 PR/branch commits on alphago-c4-solve-experiments; mirror_augment + capacity-128 was the winning composition.

title: Inline eval live curve during JAX training (blunder-rate / vs-random)
description: The JAX training loop only logs loss; eval (strength) is post-hoc via c4_certify, so wandb shows no live strength curve. Add periodic inline eval to jaxzero/train.py run_training: an eval_interval + eval that runs every N iterations and merges eval metrics into the per-iteration dict (streamed via the existing on_iteration callback). Cheap signal every interval: vs-random win-rate (reuse play_match from elo_ladder + a JaxMCTSAgent/argmax-policy agent on connectfour); optionally a small c4_certify blunder-rate sample (a handful of solver positions) less frequently. Keep eval fast so it doesn't dominate training. Result: live strength curve in wandb during the run, not just loss. Builds on the streaming-logging fix (alphago-fpz).
close_reason: Inline vs-random eval: jaxzero/evaluate.py (pgx-native greedy-vs-random, batched, no new deps); run_training logs eval/vs_random_{win,draw,loss}_rate every --eval-interval iters via on_iteration. Verified curve climbs with learning (0.78->0.88); 14 tests + gate green

Deliverable: GPU games/sec at large batch + a go/no-go.

title: JAX+pgx+mctx Connect Four self-play spike (throughput benchmark)
description: Research spike (separate, non-disruptive): build a minimal JAX self-play loop for Connect Four using pgx (connect_four env) + mctx (Gumbel AlphaZero) + a small Flax/Optax net, and benchmark self-play throughput (games/sec, env-steps/sec) head-to-head against the current PyTorch campaign (run 7jlfmjgu logs self_play_games_per_sec). Goal: a real apples-to-apples speed number to decide whether a JAX 'scale Go' track is worth it. Keep it isolated in experiments/ with its own deps (do NOT add jax/pgx/mctx to the main project). Deliverable: throughput comparison + a go/no-go writeup. Pedagogical PyTorch repo stays the reference implementation.
close_reason: JAX+pgx+mctx C4 throughput spike; CPU parity (2.3 vs 2.08 games/s); merged in #31

title: Incremental/bitboard win-check for Connect Four
description: Profiling (pane 1826 benchmark) shows alphazero/games/connectfour.py winner() is ~35 percent of self-play non-inference time: a full-board O(rows*cols*dirs) scan called ~23k times, once per MCTS node via is_terminal. Replace it with a fast bitboard win detection that preserves EXACT winner() semantics (returns +1, -1, 0, or None identically). Recommended approach: build a per-player bitboard from the board and check four-in-a-row via shift operations (mirror the technique in alphazero/c4_solver.py _has_won), keeping the winner(state) signature and behavior identical and NOT changing the ConnectFourState shape (so direct constructions in tests do not break). Add a parity test asserting the fast winner matches the previous scan across many random and terminal positions. This speeds up ALL Connect Four self-play, MCTS, and eval, not just the campaign. File scope: alphazero/games/connectfour.py and tests/test_connectfour.py only.
close_reason: Bitboard winner() for Connect Four; parity verified (8826 positions, 0 mismatches vs scan); merged in #29

title: C4 training campaign to zero blunders
description: Run the real C4 training campaign on Modal (isolated container, parallel self-play), tracking the solver-anchor + certification metrics as the headline curve, iterating network size / sims / exploration until blunder-rate hits 0 on the certification harness. Blocked by the solver extension and certification harness. Orchestrated (launched + monitored), not a code task.
close_reason: CONCLUDED (zero not reached). The campaign ran extensively (PR #46 goal: 0.082->0.036 single-seed via jla+5 levers; this session: transformer arch matching ResNet + systematic lever sweeps). Blunder floor is bounded at ~0.04-0.07 weak (net+MCTS @ sims=800) and is robust to every algorithmic lever tested (architecture, value-head, eval/self-play search, training length, batch size). Literal zero blunders was NOT achieved and requires a SOTA-scale compute run (calibrated ~3 hr / ~$6-12 per run; batched+cached cert prototyped in PR #48), not another knob. Closing as concluded; reopen / file a new bead if pursuing the compute-scale run. See memories c4-solve-target-eval-sims + c4-blunder-floor-is-data-limited.

title: Parallelize self-play across CPU cores + set Modal cpu=
description: Self-play games run sequentially in train_agent (alphazero/arena.py: 'for _ in range(self_play_games_per_iteration): play_game(...)'), so games/sec is low and CPU-bound on NN inference (go/gomoku ~0.3 games/sec isolated on Modal). The N games per iteration are independent (each gets its own game_seed) so they are embarrassingly parallel. Parallelize self-play across CPU cores (process pool; each worker rebuilds the net from state_dict, runs its share of games with per-game seeds, returns examples). Also set cpu= on the modal_app @app.function (currently unset -> small default). Keep determinism, preserve interfaces, make it configurable + fall back to sequential, add tests. No GPU needed.
close_reason: Parallel self-play + Modal cpu= merged in #22

title: Benchmark + profile training to find bottlenecks and measure speedups
description: Add a benchmarking/profiling harness to identify where training time goes and quantify speedup options. Scope: (1) per-component wall-clock breakdown per iteration -- self-play/MCTS vs network inference vs train step vs eval (gating/ladder/solver) -- exposed as metrics (and optionally to wandb). (2) A profiling mode (cProfile and/or torch.profiler; py-spy notes) to find hotspots. (3) A standalone benchmark script (e.g. alphazero/benchmark.py or scripts/) that runs a fixed workload and reports the breakdown + nodes/sec, games/sec, net-evals/sec. (4) Identify the dominant cost (expected: sequential single-position MCTS net inference) and measure candidate speedups: batched/leaf-parallel MCTS, vectorized/parallel self-play, torch eval()/no_grad/inference_mode, intra-op thread settings, float32 vs half, optional GPU batching. (5) Document findings (a short docs note or README section) and which levers help. INFRA/eval-only -- must not change learning behavior or results. Good first step before scaling to Connect Four-at-strength / larger games.
close_reason: Benchmark harness merged (#6); confirmed dominant cost = sequential MCTS net inference (~68-74%), motivates batched-MCTS bead alphago-7ck

title: Solver-backed C4 eval anchor (value-MAE, policy-match, blunder rate)

title: Optimize parallel self-play: persistent pool + round-robin chunking
description: Parallel self-play (alphago-wrp, #22) gives ~2.9x on gomoku self-play but only ~1.7x on go, ~1.8x end-to-end. Two limits: (1) the process pool is created and torn down every iteration (each iteration spawns N torch processes ~1-2s each) -> reuse a persistent pool across iterations to amortize spawn cost. (2) games are split into contiguous chunks, so with few games over many workers, go's high game-length variance causes load imbalance (one long game stalls a worker while others idle) -> use round-robin/dynamic dispatch (e.g. imap_unordered over per-game tasks) so all workers stay busy. Baseline: gomoku 0.22->0.64 self-play games/sec (1->8 workers), go 0.22->0.37. Keep determinism (parallel == sequential examples).
close_reason: Persistent pool + per-game dynamic dispatch; determinism verified across iterations; merged in #23
